title: Development of an Anatomic Landmark-Based Measurement of the Achilles Tendon
author: Daniel Urness
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.beaejabe

steps.instruction: 1. Position subject sitting upright with hip flexed, abducted, and externally rotated so the lateral aspect of the lower leg lays flat over the outline of the framing square 2. Align the plantar aspect of the foot with the base of the framing square and the posterior aspect of the calf with the vertical portion of the square to make a 90 degree angle at the ankle joint.

1.With subject positioned as above, palpate the step off where the medial head of the gastrocnemius becomes the Achilles tendon. 2. Mark above location with marking pen. 3. Using a tape measure, measure from that mark to the base of the non-compressed heel pad and record 4. Remove marking pen with alcohol pad and repeat for 3 measurements on both lower legs for a total of 6 measurements. 5. Rotate subjects to next rater until each rater has recorded measurements for each subject.

1. With the subject positioned identically to the anatomic landmark-based technique, use the ultrasound to identify the confluence of the medial head of the gastrocnemius and the Achilles tendon. 2. Mark this landmark. (The authors used a paper clip to mark this location on the ultrasound and then marked the area with a marking pen) 3. Using a tape measure, measure from that mark to the base of the non-compressed heel pad and record. 4. Remove marking pen with alcohol pad and repeat for 3 measurements on both lower legs for a total of 6 measurements. 5. Rotate subjects to next rater until each rater has recorded measurements for each subject.

1. Position patient supine with bilateral ankles at 90 degrees in MRI scanner. 2.Image must capture medial head of the gastrocnemius and distalmost portion of the non-compressed heelpad. 3. Obtain axial, coronal, and sagittal T1 series for bilateral legs.

1. Identify confluence of the medial head of the gastrocnemius with the Achilles tendon using an axial cut and identify corresponding landmark on a sagittal or coronal image. 2. Using either coronal or sagittal cut, identify distalmost portion of the non-compressed heelpad. 3. Measure the distance between the two above landmarks using imaging software. 4. Each measurement is made once on each leg by two separate raters.
